lessonTitle: Advocacy in Sickle Cell Disease: An Essential Ingredient for Quality Care

questions.question: My knowledge about Sustainable Advocacy in Sickle Cell Disease on a scale of 1 to 10 is ___ .
My comfort level doing Sustainable Advocacy for individuals with SCD on a scale of 1 to 10 is ___ .
My confidence level with maintaining Sustainable Advocacy for people with SCD on a scale of 1 to 10 is ___ .

items.html: Define advocacy for people with Sickle Cell Disease
Identify opportunities for advocacy in various time points on a SCD patients healthcare journey
Appreciate the experience of SCD patients now and what they want for the future

slides.title: Welcome message from: Mrs. Lanre Tunji-Ajayi, President/CEO of Sickle Cell Awareness Group of Ontario

data.caption: Press play to watch the video.

data.html:
While modern definitions of advocacy often refer to promotion of a cause or belief system, origins of this term refer to coming alongside another person to provide aid or support.

As healthcare practitioners, you provide aid and assistance in a variety of ways to patients and families each day.

images.caption: For people with SCD, the need for support beyond everyday medical care, check ups, and check-ins, is critical.
In addition to increased risk of marginalization due to physical health challenges, patients with SCD are more likely to experience the negative impact of discrimination.
This is based on racial background and its intersections, such as class and socioeconomic status.
When this discrimination is experienced in the healthcare setting, much more than physical health is at risk.

items.html: Psychological impact, such as increased anxiety and concerns about their safety at all points during the medical encounter and even prior to arrival in hospital.
Increased mistrust in the medical system and future avoidance of health care settings.
Prolonged pain and needless suffering that could have been addressed promptly and effectively by an alert and compassionate healthcare provider.
Worsening of physical symptoms and undertreatment of serious health complications because presenting concerns were not believed.
Delayed presentation to hospital for urgent care because negative interactions with the healthcare team are anticipated.

slides.title: Advocacy for Patients with Sickle Cell Disease

data.html:
Advocacy for patients with SCD is critical because health care teams largely lack knowledge about this condition and effective ways to treat it.

There is also a significant power imbalance built into the medical encounter.

Patients attempting to advocate for themselves by requesting particular treatments or treatment steps, especially in the throes of a pain crisis or complication, may find that their attempts are met with negativity by the healthcare team and may also find themselves labelled accordingly as demanding, drug-seeking, bossy, or rude.
This only compounds patient distress and concern.

slides.title: Dr. Carlton Haywood, a researcher who lived (1976 - 2021) with SCD, speaks to the problem and our opportunities

data.caption: Press play to watch the video.

images.caption: When healthcare providers focus on a patient's race, ability, or other indicators ahead of their need for timely assistance and support, negative outcomes are certain.

Sickle cell pain is not visible to the eye, so it is imperative for team members to be knowledgeable about SCD and associated complexities, the risk introduced by bias and anti-Black racism, and the vulnerability of sickle cell patients in the healthcare encounter.

To reduce the extent to which people with SCD are subject to stereotypes based on their race or disease status, they must be included in discussions about their care and the decision making process, to engender trust between people with SCD and their healthcare team members.
Healthcare team members must act quickly, decisively, collaboratively, and with compassion to bring about optimal outcomes for their patients.

data.html: They may do this in a variety of ways, not limited to:

images.caption: Understanding bias inherent in healthcare, what it looks like, sounds like, and how to mitigate its impact through action.
Acting on existing care protocols and using their knowledge of Ontario Health Quality Standards to provide immediate support in the Emergency Room.
Being collaborative and accommodating of patient preferences for the way that they receive care.
Validating a patient's reports of pain and conveying this to staff attending to the patient.
Connecting with a patient on the ward, and asking how they may make them more comfortable.

data.question: What is one aspect of patient experience that can benefit from advocacy in the health care setting?
data.feedback_correct: Timely treatment of pain is a crucial aspect of patient experience that can significantly benefit from advocacy in the healthcare setting.

images.caption: Advocacy is therefore informed, intentional, timely, and collaborative by nature.

It recognizes that inequities exist within healthcare and other systems and the harm that this has historically caused and continues to cause, along intersections of race, class, identity, language and more.
Advocacy is motivated by a simple wish to make things better for patients.

It is built upon positive regard for patients, assumes their good will, and commits to protecting their inherent worth and dignity by incorporating their choices and lived experience in decision-making.

With your support as a healthcare practitioner who is well-informed about sickle cell disease and committed to collaborative, compassionate care, more positive outcomes are assured for patients and families.

data.question: Which of the following resources can practitioners access to enhance advocacy for patients with sickle cell disease? (Select all that apply.)
options: Ontario Health Quality Standards for Sickle Cell Disease
Consultation with practitioners at specialized centers
Patient focused online groups and forums
data.correct_answer: Ontario Health Quality Standards for Sickle Cell Disease; Review patient's chart/history/care plan; Ask patient/caregiver; Patient support organizations like SCAGO; Consultation with practitioners at specialized centers
data.feedback_correct: Accessing resources that are reliable, evidence based, or based on the lived experience of your patient, will help you to align with best practices in advocating for the care of people with SCD.

data.question: How can your advocacy reduce the impact of bias or discrimination in the patient encounter or within subsequent care encounters? (Select all that apply.)
options: Ensuring equal distribution of hospital resources
data.correct_answer: Centering patient voice; Reducing unnecessary suffering/harm; Creating trust; Reducing risk of complications
data.feedback_correct: Through sustained and active advocacy, as a healthcare professional you can leverage your positionality to reduce the impact of bias or discrimination in the patient encounter.

items.html: Advocacy is not something that is done some of the time. Sustainable advocacy is what is needed for people with SCD.
People with Sickle Cell Disease experience bias and discrimination in their healthcare encounters, which reinforces the need for us as partners and advocates.
Being collaborative, validating experiences, and actively listening to people with SCD will benefit your patient and also you.

CMPA, n.d. Advocacy for change: An important role to undertake with care. Retrieved from: https://www.cmpa-acpm.ca/en/advice-publications/browse-articles/2014/advocacy-for-change-an-important-role-to-undertake-with-care
Haywood, C. (2013). Disrespectful care in the treatment of sickle cell disease requires more than ethics consultation. American Journal of Bioethics, 13(4):12-4

John Hopkins Berman Institute of Bioethics. n.d. Sickle Cell Disease Module They Don't Believe Me. Retrieved from https://bioethics.jhu.edu/research-and-outreach/projects/vision-of-hope/sickle-cell-disease-modules/they-dont-believe-me/
